summary: The three layers of a blood vessel wall are tunica intima, tunica media, and tunica adventitia.
The three layers are tunica mucosa, tunica muscularis, and tunica serosa.
The three layers are endothelium, elastic lamina, and connective tissue.
Elastic arteries exhibit the Windkessel function, stretching during systole and recoiling during diastole.
Muscular arteries exhibit the Windkessel function for continuous flow.
The Windkessel function refers to the valvular mechanism in veins.
Arterioles are resistance vessels and the main determinants of peripheral vascular resistance.
Arterioles are capacitance vessels that store blood.
Capillaries are the main resistance vessels in the circulation.
An end artery has no anastomoses, so its blockage causes infarction of the supplied tissue.
An end artery has abundant anastomoses providing collateral circulation.
End arteries are found only in the liver and spleen.
The ductus arteriosus shunts blood from the pulmonary trunk to the aorta in fetal circulation.
The ductus arteriosus shunts blood from the aorta to the pulmonary trunk.
The foramen ovale shunts blood from the pulmonary trunk to the aorta.
Veins are capacitance vessels containing valves to prevent backflow, holding about 70% of blood volume.
Arteries are capacitance vessels holding most of the blood volume.
Veins hold approximately 30% of the total blood volume.
Vasa vasorum are small vessels that supply the walls of large arteries and veins.
Vasa vasorum are the main branches of the aorta.
Vasa vasorum are specialised capillaries found only in the brain.
